result:
I.

There is a moment—usually around 3:17 a.m.—when the city forgets itself. The traffic lights keep cycling red-amber-green for an audience of one or two stray taxis, and the neon signs blink their syllables in half-patterns that no longer spell anything intelligible. It is the hour when the municipal loudspeakers stop preaching about recycling, when the smell of yeast from the all-night bakery finally overpowers the diesel, when even the dogs stop pretending to be wolves and simply sit on the curb, tongues out, puzzled by their own existence. I have come to think of 3:17 as the hinge of the day: lift it gently and the entire contraption of obligations, receipts, passwords, dentist appointments and love affairs swings open like a suitcase, revealing the strange socks tucked inside.

I used to fear that hour. I thought insomnia was a moral defect, evidence of slothful neurotransmitters or a mis-calibrated soul. Then, sometime in my thirty-third year, I discovered that if I walked out the door with no phone, no watch, no house key (the lock is broken anyway), the city would loan me a temporary citizenship in a shadow nation that never appears on maps. The first article of its constitution: you are not required to be interesting. The second: anything you touch may touch you back. The third: time is allowed to ooze sideways, like watercolor on wet paper. Under those rules I have watched the rivets on a bridge breathe in and out, have conversed with an elderly man who claimed he was translating the I Ching into cigarette smoke, have helped a lost tourist return a sunset to the ocean because “it did not fit in my suitcase.”

Tonight I leave the apartment quietly, shoes in hand so the staircase dog doesn’t bark. The stairwell smells of fried cumin and cheap disinfectant, a pairing no perfumer would dare. On the landing someone has scrawled, in what looks like lipstick the color of emergency, the words: “The mirror is only as honest as your last apology.” I pause, wondering whether to write back, but the stick of chalk I keep for such replies has crumbled to dust in my pocket. Instead I trace the sentence with my finger, transferring half the pigment to my skin, a red oath I will carry to the street.

II.

Outside, the air is the temperature of unresolved longing. A single bat threads the corridor between buildings, its sonar pinging off aluminum shutters. I follow it instinctively, the way a moth follows an unspoken theorem about light. We emerge onto the riverside boulevard, where a lone food cart advertises “Vietnamese tacos—3 for the price of yesterday.” The vendor wears a felt hat shaped like a fortune cookie and refuses to meet anyone’s gaze. His right hand stirs a vat of something that smells like star anise deciding to immigrate to Mexico. I buy three tacos because it feels ceremonial. He wraps them in a page torn from an old encyclopedia; I catch the phrase “…the disco clam produces flashes of light by rolling reflective silica spheres….” I imagine the clam on a tiny dance floor, mirror ball overhead, bivalves swaying under stroboscopic seduction. The image pleases me so much I almost forget to pay, but the vendor simply points to a hand-lettered sign: AFTER 3 A.M., PAY IN STORIES. So I tell him about the clam discotheque. He nods solemnly, folds the story into a paper crane, and pins it to a string already decorated with dozens of other paper birds. Each crane, I realize, is somebody’s 3 a.m. currency: love affairs gone septic, childhood memories rewound too far, jokes that died on stage. The line of cranes flutters like a spine made of whispered secrets.

III.

I eat two tacos and offer the third to the bat, who declines but thanks me in ultrasonic Morse. While I chew, a woman approaches wearing a coat made entirely of pockets. Dozens of pockets, of every conceivable size: coin, envelope, breadbox, bathtub. From one she produces a deck of tarot cards that are transparent except for the edges. “Pick,” she says. I draw the Tower, but instead of the usual lightning it depicts a high-rise elevator whose cables have turned into spaghetti. Passengers float calmly, fork in hand. “You’re already in free fall,” she observes, “so the only question is whether you’ll season the journey.” She refuses payment, but takes a Polaroid of my left earlobe “for archival purposes,” then vanishes toward the river.

IV.

Thus begins the parade. At 3:17 a.m. the city loosens its seams and things slip through: a brass band of retired accountants who play only diminished chords; a trio of skateboarding grandmothers rehearsing Macbeth; a courier delivering silence in shrink-wrapped parcels; a child walking an invisible dinosaur that leaves wet footprints of condensation. No one marvels; marveling is a daytime occupation. Instead we nod, exchange the small change of wonder, move on. I think of these apparitions as nightly reparations for the hours we spend soldered to spreadsheets, headlines, the slow drip of consensus reality. They are not hallucinations; hallucinations are when you see what isn’t there. These are visitations from what is there all the time but refuses to file tax returns.

V.

Somewhere near the old planetarium I encounter a man painting constellations on the sidewalk with a brush dipped in liquid phosphorus. He works quickly, as though pursued by dawn. “The stars are forgetting their positions,” he explains without looking up. “Someone has to file a backup.” I watch him dot Betelgeuse into existence near a sewer grate, sketch the entire Southern Cross where it will be trampled by office clerks at 8 a.m. He gifts me a small glass vial labeled “excess darkness,” advises me to sprinkle it behind the ears before job interviews. I tuck it into the same pocket where my phone used to live, the phone I have deliberately left home because screens are tiny black holes that inhale coincidence.

VI.

We walk together, the painter and I, until we reach the shuttered opera house. Its marquee still bears the letters of last season’s failed production: “CAR—EN—S—Z—E.” Random bulbs blink like a stroke victim trying to speak. The stage door is ajar; inside, the darkness smells of velvet and abandoned arpeggios. We ascend the grand staircase, footsteps triggering ghost chords from the carpet. On stage, a single rehearsing light burns. Beneath it stands a dressmaker’s dummy wearing a gown made of unread letters. The painter bows as if greeting royalty, then climbs into the orchestra pit and begins to tune the kettledrums with a set of wrenches. I approach the dummy. Up close I see the letters are addressed to me: every postcard I never sent, every apology I drafted and deleted, every love letter that arrived too late. The paper rustles anxiously, wanting to be read aloud. I hesitate; some envelopes remain sealed for good reason. But 3:17 is an hour that forgives exposure. I open one at random: “Dear You, I kept the record of your breathing from the night you fell asleep on my shoulder. I play it backwards whenever the walls begin to echo.” Another: “I forgave you in a language you don’t speak.” Letter after letter slips through my fingers, each confessing a tenderness I had misplaced like loose change. By the time I reach the final envelope, the gown has deflated into a puddle of paper. I stand ankle-deep in my own unspoken history, feeling both lighter and newly accountable.

VII.

From the pit rises a low rumble: the painter has coaxed a chord out of the timpani that sounds exactly like a sunrise heard from underwater. The resonance loosens something in the rafters; a rain of theater masks falls—comedy, tragedy, and several expressions for which the Greeks never found verbs. I pick one that is simply a circle: no mouth, two subtle dimples suggesting pupils. When I press it to my face, the auditorium rearranges itself into a childhood bedroom. Posters of galaxies thumbtacked to the ceiling, glow-in-the-dark stars spelling a name I outgrew. My mother knocks, asks if I’m still awake. She looks the same age as I am now; time has folded us together like a letter mailed to itself. She sits on the edge of the bed, holds out her hand. In her palm rests the key to the apartment I left tonight. “Locks change,” she says, “but the door remembers.” I try to ask her what she means but the mask slips, and I’m back on the opera stage, alone except for the painter who is now wrapping the timpani in blackout curtains, tucking them in like infants.

VIII.

Dawn is incompetent in this part of the world; it needs rehearsal. Nevertheless, the sky has begun to bruise lilac. Citizens of 3:17 begin to evaporate: the bat veers toward a belfry, the skateboarding witches kick-flip into fog, the man who sells silence drives off in a van that makes no sound, not even of tires. I retrieve my shoes, now covered in phosphorescent star-dust, and walk home along the river. The water’s surface is a rough draft of the sky, full of cross-outs and marginalia. Under the bridge someone has built a small cairn of cigarette filters, each white tip painted with a single letter. Together they spell: “Almost was enough.” I add a filter I find in my pocket—leftover from a former life—painting on the word “again.”

IX.

Back at the apartment, the staircase dog is asleep, snoring in 5/4 time. I enter quietly, replace the broken key on the hook, a gesture like ending a sentence with a semicolon. The first birds begin tentative arpeggios outside. I sit at the kitchen table, unwrap the final taco from the encyclopedia page. The article underneath is about the axolotl, the Mexican salamander that refuses to grow up. Neoteny, scientists call it: the retention of juvenile features into adulthood. I chew slowly, thinking about the city at 3:17, about my own stalled metamorphosis. Perhaps insomnia is not a disorder but a form of neoteny; perhaps the night simply refuses to graduate into day. I finish the taco, lick the last of the star-anise salsa from my fingers, and realize I am unspeakably calm.

X.

I open the vial of “excess darkness” and pour it into a glass of water. It disperses like cuttlefish ink, then reorganizes into tiny constellations. Drinking it tastes like closing a book you thought you’d never finish. I carry the glass to the window. Across the street, the early-shift baker is hauling trays of dough. He sees me, raises a hand dusted in flour, a white constellation against his apron. We nod, fellow conspirators of the almost-dawn. Somewhere in his ovens, yeast is exhaling; somewhere in my bloodstream, the dark water is mapping new coastlines.

XI.

In bed, I wait for the click when 3:17 closes its suitcase. It comes—not as a sound, but as a soft pressure behind the eyes, the way a camera lens settles into focus. I set the alarm anyway, out of habit. The numbers glow 6:30, a time that now feels like a foreign country whose currency I no longer carry. I lie back, palm open, the faint red echo of the staircase graffiti still staining my skin. The mirror above the dresser catches my reflection, but for once it does not ask for an apology. Instead it shows the city folded inside my iris: cranes made of stories, phosphorescent sidewalks, a disco clam spinning light into spaghetti cables. I watch until the image blurs, not from tears but from the understanding that I have been awake long enough to finally dream with my eyes open.

XII.

When sleep arrives, it does not descend like a curtain; it rises like water, filling the room from floor to ceiling. I float in it, neutrally buoyant, the way astronauts train in pools. Outside, daytime begins its confident rehearsal: horns, headlines, micro-aggressions, deadlines. Inside, I drift past the submerged letters, the orphaned cranes, the kettledrum sunrise. A bat taps against the window, testing for entry. I open it—not the window, but the hour itself—hinge 3:17 wide, install it like a skylight in the ceiling of noon. People on the sidewalk below will glance up, puzzled by the slice of darkness overhead, by the faint sound of a disco ball turning. Most will shrug and keep walking. But someone, somewhere, will recognize the invitation, will step aside from the parade of necessity, will begin to climb the staircase whose dog sings in 5/4. They will arrive breathless at my door, hand raised to knock, and I—still dripping with sleep, pocket full of darkness—will greet them not as host or guest, but as cartographers of the ongoing map. Together we will unfold the suitcase of 3:17, shake out its creases, discover new countries creased inside the fabric. And there, under the neon that no longer spells anything, we will set up our folding chairs, our paper cranes, our timpani tuned to sunrise, and wait for the city to forget itself again.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
